Sunlight as a magnifier.<[silence:40ms]> Glass towers bend its fury.<[silence:40ms]>

The phrase "glass towers melt cars" comes from curved skyscrapers acting like giant lenses—focusing sunlight into scorching beams.<[silence:40ms]> A flat window scatters light harmlessly, but a concave glass facade bends rays inward, concentrating heat like a child’s magnifying glass over ants.<[silence:40ms]> In 2013, London’s Walkie-Talkie tower warped this principle into reality: its 37-story curve reflected sunlight at 117 degrees Fahrenheit, blistering car paint, cracking side mirrors, and even singeing a journalist’s trousers.<[silence:40ms]> Engineers call this the "death ray effect"—a misfire of physics, not malice.<[silence:40ms]>

clean_story:
Sunlight as a magnifier. Glass towers bend its fury.

The phrase "glass towers melt cars" comes from curved skyscrapers acting like giant lenses—focusing sunlight into scorching beams. A flat window scatters light harmlessly, but a concave glass facade bends rays inward, concentrating heat like a child’s magnifying glass over ants. In 2013, London’s Walkie-Talkie tower warped this principle into reality: its 37-story curve reflected sunlight at 117 degrees Fahrenheit, blistering car paint, cracking side mirrors, and even singeing a journalist’s trousers. Engineers call this the "death ray effect"—a misfire of physics, not malice.

The mechanism is simple: sunlight hits the curve, bounces at precise angles, and converges into a hotspot. Like a satellite dish catching radio waves, but with fire. Solutions? Brise-soleil (sun-breaking fins), anti-reflective coatings, or—after lawsuits—temporary scaffolding shades. Yet the lesson lingers: architecture must outthink the sun.

Three related subjects are urban heat islands, solar furnace designs, and how prisms split light.
